steps.title: Vroeger...
steps.desc: You already know the perfect tense (ik heb gedaan, ik ben gegaan). Now meet its partner: the imperfectum (simple past). We start with the two most important verbs: zijn → was/waren and hebben → had/hadden. These two are so common you'll learn them as vocabulary, not as grammar.
goals: 'Vroeger' = in the past / back in the day
Talk about how things USED to be

steps.title: 📖 Perfect vs Imperfectum — Preview
steps.text:
You already know the perfect tense (Unit 7):
Ik heb gegeten. = I have eaten. / I ate.
Ik ben gegaan. = I have gone. / I went.

Now: the imperfectum (simple past):
Ik at. = I ate.
Ik ging. = I went.

What's the difference?

Perfect = finished actions, results matter NOW.
'Ik heb gegeten.' = I've eaten. (I'm not hungry now.)

Imperfectum = stories, descriptions, background, 'used to'.
'Vroeger at ik altijd brood.' = I always used to eat bread.

Spoken Dutch uses the PERFECT for almost everything.
The imperfectum is for stories, 'vroeger', and writing.

But for zijn and hebben? The imperfectum is MORE common:
'Ik was moe.' (not 'Ik ben moe geweest.')
'Ik had honger.' (not 'Ik heb honger gehad.')

steps.en: in the past / back in the day / used to
steps.exampleEn: I used to live in Rotterdam.
steps.note:
One of the most Dutch words for nostalgia.
'Vroeger was alles beter.' = Everything used to be better.
The Dutch LOVE talking about 'vroeger'.

Singular past of zijn.
ik was, jij was, hij was, zij was.
Identical to English — easiest Dutch grammar ever.

Plural past of zijn.
wij waren, jullie waren, zij waren.
Same as English 'were' and German 'waren'.

steps.title: Zijn: imperfectum
steps.label: Simple Past
steps.note:
Two forms: was (singular) and waren (plural).
Same as English! The easiest Dutch past tense.

steps.exampleEn: I was hungry. (lit: I had hunger.)
steps.note:
Singular past of hebben.
ik had, jij had, hij had.
Identical to English.

Plural past of hebben.
Double 'd' keeps the 'a' short.
wij hadden, jullie hadden, zij hadden.

steps.title: Hebben: imperfectum
steps.label: Simple Past
steps.note:
Two forms: had (singular) and hadden (plural).
Same as English! was/were + had/hadden — you already know these.

steps.exampleEn: Back then I was still young.
steps.note:
'Toen' = then (past). NOT 'dan' (dan = then, future).
'Toen ik jong was...' = When I was young...
Very common in stories and 'vroeger' talk.

steps.example: Ik was toen nog een kind.
steps.exampleEn: I was still a child back then.
steps.note:
Tiny word, big meaning.
'Nog niet' = not yet.
'Nog steeds' = still (continuing).

steps.title: 🇳🇱 'Vroeger Was Alles Beter'
steps.text:
The Dutch love nostalgia — and they'll tell you about it.

'Vroeger was alles beter.' = Everything used to be better.
This is said with a mix of irony and sincerity.

Vroeger was het brood beter. = The bread used to be better.
Vroeger was het niet zo duur. = It wasn't so expensive before.
Vroeger hadden wij geen mobiel. = We didn't have phones before.

The word 'vroeger' triggers a flood of memories.
It's the Dutch equivalent of 'back in my day...'

You might hear: 'Vroeger was hier allemaal water.'
= This used to be all water.
(In the Netherlands, this is often literally true.)

steps.s: Vroeger {1} ik moe. Wij {2} een grote tuin.
steps.hint: I used to be tired... We had...

steps.q: For zijn and hebben, the Dutch prefer:
steps.hint: 'Ik was moe' is more natural than 'Ik ben moe geweest'

steps.q: 'Vroeger was alles beter' is a Dutch expression of:
steps.hint: Back in the day everything was better...

steps.title: Ik Werkte, Ik Fietste
steps.desc: Now the system: how to form the imperfectum for REGULAR verbs. The rule uses 't kofschip — the same mnemonic from Unit 7. If the stam ends in t/k/f/s/ch/p → add -te. Otherwise → add -de. Singular = stam + te/de. Plural = stam + ten/den.
goals: Form the past tense of any regular verb

steps.title: ⚓ 't Kofschip Returns!
steps.text:
In Unit 7 you learned 't kofschip for past participles:
ge- + stam + t (if stam ends in t, k, f, s, ch, p)
ge- + stam + d (otherwise)

The SAME rule applies to the imperfectum!

If the stam ends in t, k, f, s, ch, p → add -TE / -TEN
Otherwise → add -DE / -DEN

werken → stam 'werk' → k is in 't kofschip → werkte / werkten
fietsen → stam 'fiets' → s is in 't kofschip → fietste / fietsten
wonen → stam 'woon' → n is NOT in 't kofschip → woonde / woonden

⚠️ Remember the V/Z trap from Unit 7!
leven → stam 'leef' → looks like F (kofschip)...
But the INFINITIVE has V (le-ven). V is NOT in 't kofschip.
So: leefDE, not leefTE. Same rule as participles: check the infinitive!
reizen → stam 'reis' → looks like S (kofschip)...
But the INFINITIVE has Z (rei-zen). Z is NOT in 't kofschip.
So: reisDE, not reisTE.

steps.note:
werk + te = werkte.
'k' is in 't kofschip → -te.
ik werkte, jij werkte, hij werkte.

werk + ten = werkten.
Plural just adds -n to the singular form.
wij werkten, jullie werkten, zij werkten.

fiets + te = fietste.
's' is in 't kofschip → -te.
Straightforward: stam 'fiets' + ending '-te'.

woon + de = woonde.
'n' is NOT in 't kofschip → -de.
ik woonde, jij woonde, hij woonde.

steps.example: Wij woonden in een groot huis.
steps.exampleEn: We lived in a big house.
steps.note:
woon + den = woonden.
Plural adds -n: woonde → woonden.

leer + de = leerde.
'r' is NOT in 't kofschip → -de.
Also means 'taught': Hij leerde mij Nederlands.

steps.title: 📐 The Full System
steps.text:
SINGULAR (ik, jij, hij, zij, het, u):
Stam + te (if last letter is in 't kofschip)
Stam + de (otherwise)

PLURAL (wij, jullie, zij):
Stam + ten (if last letter is in 't kofschip)
Stam + den (otherwise)

Examples:
werken → werk → werkte / werkten (k = kofschip)
koken → kook → kookte / kookten (k = kofschip)
fietsen → fiets → fietste / fietsten (s = kofschip)
wonen → woon → woonde / woonden (n ≠ kofschip)
leren → leer → leerde / leerden (r ≠ kofschip)
spelen → speel → speelde / speelden (l ≠ kofschip)

⚠️ THE V/Z TRAP (same as Unit 7 participles!):
leven → stam = leef (v→f devoicing)
But check the INFINITIVE: le-VEN → V is NOT kofschip → leefDE ✅
reizen → stam = reis (z→s devoicing)
But check the INFINITIVE: rei-ZEN → Z is NOT kofschip → reisDE ✅

Rule: if the infinitive has V or Z before -en,
always use -DE/-DEN, regardless of how the stam looks.

steps.note:
speel + de = speelde.
'l' is NOT in 't kofschip → -de.
Goed zo — you're getting the pattern!

kook + te = kookte.
'k' IS in 't kofschip → -te.
Vowel stays long (open syllable): kook-te.

opts: The first letter of the verb
The last letter of the stam
The last letter of the infinitive
steps.ans: The last letter of the stam
steps.hint: Check the stam's final letter against 't kofschip

steps.s: Zij {1} naar school. Wij {2} elke dag.

steps.hint: leren = both to learn AND to teach

steps.title: Ik Ging, Ik Kwam
steps.desc: Not all verbs follow 't kofschip. Strong verbs change their vowel in the past — just like English (go → went, come → came, drink → drank). Dutch does the same: gaan → ging, komen → kwam, drinken → dronk. You need to memorize these, but the good news: they often match English or German.
goals: Singular vs plural of strong verbs
Tell a simple story in the past

steps.title: ⚡ Strong Verbs: The Vowel Change
steps.text:
Regular verbs (weak): add -te/-de.
werken → werkte. wonen → woonde.

Irregular verbs (strong): the vowel CHANGES.
gaan → ging. komen → kwam. drinken → dronk.

English does this too:
go → went. come → came. drink → drank.

German does too:
gehen → ging. kommen → kam. trinken → trank.

The pattern:
Singular = new vowel form (ging, kwam, dronk)
Plural = new vowel + en (gingen, kwamen, dronken)

You can't predict which vowel — you memorize them.
But many MATCH English or German, so you have a head start.

steps.note:
gaan → ging (singular), gingen (plural).
Identical to German: ging / gingen.
ik ging, wij gingen.

komen → kwam (singular), kwamen (plural).
Same as German: kam / kamen.
English: came (same vowel shift!).

drinken → dronk / dronken.
English: drink → drank. Same shift!
German: trinken → trank.

eten → at (singular), aten (plural).
English: eat → ate. Same root!
ik at, wij aten.

doen → deed (singular), deden (plural).
English: do → did.
'Wat deed je?' = What did you do?

zien → zag (singular), zagen (plural).
English: see → saw.
German: sehen → sah.

geven → gaf (singular), gaven (plural).
English: give → gave. Identical shift!
Note: f in singular, v in plural (voicing).

nemen → nam (singular), namen (plural).
German: nehmen → nahm. Almost identical!
English went a different direction: take → took.

staan → stond (singular), stonden (plural).
English: stand → stood. Same root!
ik stond, wij stonden.

schrijven → schreef / schreven.
German: schreiben → schrieb. Same pattern!
Note: f in singular, v in plural (voicing again).

steps.title: 📋 The 10 You Need Most
steps.text:
gaan → ging / gingen = went
komen → kwam / kwamen = came
eten → at / aten = ate
drinken → dronk / dronken = drank
doen → deed / deden = did
zien → zag / zagen = saw
geven → gaf / gaven = gave
nemen → nam / namen = took
staan → stond / stonden = stood
schrijven → schreef / schreven = wrote

Pattern: singular = vowel change.
Plural = singular + en.

⚠️ Some have f/v alternation in singular/plural:
gaf → gaven, schreef → schreven.
Same voicing rule as plurals (huis → huizen).

steps.hint: Singular + en = plural for strong verbs
Same as glas → glazen, huis → huizen

steps.title: Heb Gedaan vs Deed
steps.desc: When do you use 'ik heb gedaan' (perfect) and when 'ik deed' (imperfectum)? In spoken Dutch, the perfect dominates for almost everything. The imperfectum has specific jobs: stories, descriptions, 'used to', and zijn/hebben. This lesson clarifies when to use which.
goals: When to use the perfect tense

steps.title: ⚖️ The Big Difference
steps.text:
PERFECT TENSE (voltooid tegenwoordige tijd):
Ik heb gegeten. = I have eaten. / I ate.
→ Finished action, result matters NOW.
→ Default tense in SPOKEN Dutch.
→ 'Ik heb gisteren gewerkt.' = I worked yesterday.

IMPERFECTUM (onvoltooid verleden tijd):
Ik at. = I ate.
→ Stories, background, descriptions, 'used to'.
→ Default tense in WRITTEN Dutch / stories.
→ 'Vroeger at ik altijd brood.' = I used to always eat bread.

The key insight:
Spoken Dutch = mostly perfect.
Written Dutch / stories = more imperfectum.
Sijn/hebben = almost always imperfectum (was/had).

steps.title: 📐 When to Use Which
steps.text:
USE THE PERFECT when:
✅ Talking about recent events
  Ik heb gisteren gewerkt. = I worked yesterday.
✅ Stating completed actions
  Ik heb het gedaan. = I've done it.
✅ In normal conversation
  Heb je gegeten? = Have you eaten?

USE THE IMPERFECTUM when:
✅ Telling stories
  Er was eens een prins... = Once upon a time there was a prince...
✅ Describing background/state
  Het was koud. De straat was leeg. = It was cold. The street was empty.
✅ 'Used to' (vroeger/altijd)
  Vroeger woonde ik in Utrecht. = I used to live in Utrecht.
✅ zijn and hebben (always)
  Ik was moe. Ik had honger. (NOT: ik ben moe geweest)
✅ Modal verbs (often)
  Ik kon niet komen. = I couldn't come.
  Ik moest werken. = I had to work.

deepDive.title: The modal imperfectums
deepDive.text:
Modal verbs almost always use the imperfectum:

willen → wilde / wilden (wanted)
kunnen → kon / konden (could)
mogen → mocht / mochten (was allowed to)
moeten → moest / moesten (had to)

Ik wilde komen. = I wanted to come.
Ik kon niet fietsen. = I couldn't cycle.
Ik mocht niet roken. = I wasn't allowed to smoke.
Ik moest werken. = I had to work.

These are HIGH-frequency forms — you'll hear them daily.

steps.note:
willen → wilde (singular), wilden (plural).
Regular -de ending. Very common.
'Ik wilde niet.' = I didn't want to.

kunnen → kon (singular), konden (plural).
Strong verb (vowel change: u → o).
Very common: 'Ik kon niet...' = I couldn't...

moeten → moest (singular), moesten (plural).
Strong verb.
'Ik moest werken.' = I had to work.

mogen → mocht (singular), mochten (plural).
Strong verb.
'Ik mocht niet...' = I wasn't allowed to...

steps.title: 💡 The Simple Rule for Daily Life
steps.text:
When in doubt, use the PERFECT in conversation.

The only times you MUST use imperfectum:
1. zijn → was / waren
2. hebben → had / hadden
3. Modal verbs: wilde, kon, moest, mocht

For everything else, the perfect works fine:
'Ik heb gisteren gefietst.' ✅ (I cycled yesterday.)
'Ik fietste gisteren.' ✅ (Also correct, but more literary.)

Both are correct. The perfect is just more natural in speech.

For stories and writing, mix both:
'Het was een mooie dag. Ik ging naar de markt
en ik heb kaas gekocht.'
(Imperfectum for background, perfect for actions.)

steps.q: In SPOKEN Dutch, the default past tense is:
steps.hint: 'Ik heb gegeten' is more natural than 'ik at'

steps.q: 'Vroeger woonde ik in Utrecht.' uses imperfectum because:

steps.hint: I couldn't come. I had to work.

steps.q: For a story that starts 'Er was eens...', you'd use:
steps.hint: Stories and fairy tales use imperfectum

steps.q: 'Ik heb gisteren gefietst' vs 'Ik fietste gisteren' — which is more natural in speech?

steps.title: De Man Die...
steps.desc: The last major grammar point: relative clauses. 'The man WHO lives here', 'the house THAT is big.' Dutch uses 'die' for de-words and 'dat' for het-words. And like all subordinate clauses, the verb goes to the END.
goals: Verb goes to end in relative clauses

steps.title: 🔗 What Is a Relative Clause?
steps.text:
A relative clause adds information about a noun:

'The man who lives here.' → 'who lives here' = relative clause.
'The house that is big.' → 'that is big' = relative clause.

English uses: who, which, that.
Dutch uses: die or dat.

The rule is simple:
De-words → die
De man die hier woont. = The man who lives here.

Het-words → dat
Het huis dat groot is. = The house that is big.

Plurals → always die
De kinderen die spelen. = The children who are playing.

⚠️ In the relative clause, the verb goes to the END.
De man die hier WOONT. (not: die woont hier)

steps.example: De man die hier woont, is mijn buurman.
steps.exampleEn: The man who lives here is my neighbour.
steps.note:
'Die' for ALL de-words and ALL plurals.
De vrouw die werkt... = The woman who works...
De kinderen die spelen... = The children who play...

steps.example: Het boek dat ik lees, is goed.
steps.exampleEn: The book that I'm reading is good.
steps.note:
'Dat' ONLY for singular het-words.
Het kind dat speelt... = The child who plays...
Het huis dat groot is... = The house that is big...

steps.title: 📐 The Verb-Final Rule
steps.text:
In relative clauses, the conjugated verb goes to the END:

Main clause: De man woont hier.
            (The man lives here. — verb in position 2)

Relative clause: De man die hier WOONT...
                (The man who lives here... — verb at end)

More examples:
Het meisje dat Nederlands LEERT... = The girl who learns Dutch...
De fiets die ik gisteren KOCHT... = The bike that I bought yesterday...
Het eten dat zij KOOKT... = The food that she cooks...

This is the same rule as subordinate clauses (Unit 9):
omdat, dat, als, wanneer → verb to end.
Relative clauses follow the exact same pattern.

deepDive.title: Two verbs in a relative clause
deepDive.text:
When there are two verbs (modal + infinitive, or perfect tense),
both go to the end:

De man die niet KAN KOMEN... = The man who can't come...
Het boek dat ik GELEZEN HEB... = The book that I've read...

The order at the end can be:
infinitive + modal: die niet komen kan (formal)
modal + infinitive: die niet kan komen (common)

Both are correct. Don't stress about this —
at A2 level, just getting the verb to the end is the win.

steps.example: De buurman die naast ons woont.
steps.exampleEn: The neighbour who lives next to us.
steps.note:
De-word. Buur + man = neighbour-man.
Female: de buurvrouw.
The Dutch have a strong 'buren' (neighbours) culture.

steps.example: Het verhaal dat zij schreef, was mooi.
steps.exampleEn: The story that she wrote was beautiful.
steps.note:
Het-word. Plural: verhalen.
From 'verhalen' (to tell/narrate).
You'll read a short verhaal in L6!

steps.q: 'De vrouw die hier werkt' — why 'die' and not 'dat'?

steps.s: Het kind ___ buiten speelt, is mijn zoon.

steps.q: In a relative clause, the verb goes:

steps.s: De fiets {1} ik kocht, is mooi. Het eten {2} zij kookt, is lekker.
steps.hint: De fiets = de-word → die. Het eten = het-word → dat

steps.s: De man ___ naast ons woont, heet Jan.

steps.hint: De kinderen die... De huizen die...

steps.q: 'Het verhaal dat ik las, was mooi.' — 'las' is at the end because:

steps.s: De buurvrouw ___ hier woont, is aardig.

steps.title: Een Kort Verhaal
steps.desc: Time to READ Dutch. This lesson presents a short story with translations to help you. Most of the grammar and vocabulary comes from what you've already learned — plus a handful of new story words. The story uses the imperfectum for narrative, exactly how real Dutch stories work.

steps.example: Ik fiets, want het is mooi weer.
steps.exampleEn: I cycle, because it's nice weather.
steps.note:
'Want' = because, but unlike 'omdat' (Unit 9),
it keeps NORMAL word order (verb stays in position 2).
omdat: Ik fiets omdat het mooi weer IS. (verb → end)
want: Ik fiets, want het IS mooi weer. (verb stays put)
English once used 'for' the same way: 'I stayed, for it was cold.'
You might still see this in literature or formal writing.
Dutch 'want' kept this usage alive in everyday speech.

steps.title: 📖 Een Dag op de Markt
steps.text:
Read this story FIRST in Dutch only.
Try to understand as much as you can.
You know most of these words already.
New words will be taught after the story.

Het was zaterdag. De zon scheen.

Anna ging naar de markt.
Zij fietste, want het was mooi weer.

Op de markt waren veel mensen. Het was druk.

Anna wilde kaas kopen. De kaas was niet duur.

'Hoeveel kost dat?' vroeg Anna.

'Drie euro vijftig,' zei de man.

Anna kocht de kaas en een broodje.

Daarna dronk zij een kopje koffie op het terras.

Het was een goede dag.

steps.q: In the story, what did Anna want to buy?
What did Anna drink after shopping?

steps.title: 📖 Vertalingen (Translations)
steps.text:
Now check your understanding:

Het was zaterdag. De zon scheen.
→ It was Saturday. The sun was shining.

Anna ging naar de markt. Zij fietste, want het was mooi weer.
→ Anna went to the market. She cycled, because it was nice weather.

Op de markt waren veel mensen. Het was druk.
→ At the market there were many people. It was busy.

Anna wilde kaas kopen. De kaas was niet duur.
→ Anna wanted to buy cheese. The cheese wasn't expensive.

'Hoeveel kost dat?' vroeg Anna. 'Drie euro vijftig,' zei de man.
→ 'How much does that cost?' asked Anna. 'Three fifty,' said the man.

Anna kocht de kaas en een broodje.
→ Anna bought the cheese and a bread roll.

Daarna dronk zij een kopje koffie op het terras.
→ Afterwards she drank a cup of coffee on the terrace.

Het was een goede dag.
→ It was a good day.

steps.note:
schijnen → scheen (strong verb).
Used for weather: 'de zon scheen' = the sun shone.
Also: schijnen = to seem/appear.

You've seen 'hoeveel' (how much).
Now: 'veel' alone = much/many/a lot.
Veel mensen = many people.

steps.example: Het was druk op de markt.
steps.exampleEn: It was busy at the market.
steps.note:
Very Dutch word. Used for places AND people.
'Het is druk.' = It's busy/crowded.
'Ik heb het druk.' = I'm busy.

steps.exampleEn: 'How much does that cost?' she asked.
steps.note:
vragen → vroeg (strong verb).
Also means 'early' (adjective)!
'vroeg' = asked (verb) OR early (adjective). Context decides.

zeggen → zei (singular), zeiden (plural).
Very common in stories and reported speech.
Used just like English 'said'.

steps.example: Zij kocht kaas op de markt.
steps.exampleEn: She bought cheese at the market.
steps.note:
kopen → kocht (singular), kochten (plural).
Strong verb. The 'ch' is the throat sound.
Useful: 'Ik heb het gekocht.' = I bought it. (perfect)

daar + na = there + after.
Very useful time connector for stories.
'Eerst... daarna...' = First... then...

steps.title: 📐 Notice the Tense Mix
steps.text:
The story mixes imperfectum and perfect naturally:

IMPERFECTUM (background, description, state):
Het was zaterdag. = It was Saturday.
De zon scheen. = The sun was shining.
Het was druk. = It was busy.
De kaas was niet duur. = The cheese wasn't expensive.

IMPERFECTUM (story actions in narrative):
Anna ging naar de markt. = Anna went to the market.
Zij fietste. = She cycled.
Anna kocht de kaas. = Anna bought the cheese.

This is typical Dutch story-writing:
imperfectum carries the narrative.
The perfect tense would appear in dialogue.

Written Dutch stories = mostly imperfectum.
Spoken Dutch retelling = mostly perfect.

steps.q: In the story, 'Het was zaterdag' uses imperfectum because:

steps.hint: She bought cheese. Then she drank coffee.

steps.q: The story uses mostly imperfectum because:

steps.title: Mijn Week
steps.desc: Production time. You've learned two past tenses: the perfect from Unit 7 (ik heb gedaan, ik ben gegaan) and the imperfectum from this unit (ik deed, ik ging). Now put them together. Spoken Dutch uses the perfect; written Dutch uses the imperfectum. This lesson is all about YOU describing YOUR week — both ways.

steps.title: ✍️ How to Talk About Your Week
steps.text:
Spoken Dutch (casual, with friends):

Wat heb je dit weekend gedaan?
= What did you do this weekend?

Ik heb zaterdag gewerkt.
= I worked on Saturday.

Daarna ben ik naar de markt gegaan.
= Then I went to the market.

Ik heb kaas gekocht en koffie gedronken.
= I bought cheese and drank coffee.

Het was leuk!
= It was fun!

—

Written Dutch (in an email or diary):

Zaterdag werkte ik. Daarna ging ik naar de markt.
Ik kocht kaas en dronk koffie. Het was een goede dag.

Notice: spoken = perfect. Written = imperfectum.
Both describe the same events!

steps.example: Wat heb je dit weekend gedaan?
steps.exampleEn: What did you do this weekend?
steps.note:
'Dit' = this (het-word: het weekend).
The #1 Monday morning question in every Dutch office.
'Dit weekend' or 'afgelopen weekend' (past weekend).

The most common Dutch positive word.
'Leuk!' = Nice! / Cool! / Fun!
'Dat was leuk.' = That was fun.
Not as strong as 'geweldig' (amazing).

steps.example: Eerst ging ik naar de winkel.
steps.exampleEn: First I went to the shop.
steps.note:
Same root as English 'erstwhile' (= formerly).
'Erst' is Old English for 'first' — it survives
only in 'erstwhile.' Dutch kept it as the everyday word.
Time connector for sequences:
'Eerst... daarna... toen...' = First... then... then...
Very useful for telling stories.

You saw this in L6. Most common sequencer.
'Eerst' → 'daarna' → 'toen' → 'uiteindelijk'
First → then → then → finally.

Long word! uit + einde + lijk.
Uit (out) + einde (end) + -lijk (suffix) = in the end.
Used to wrap up a story.

steps.title: 📐 Time Connectors for Stories
steps.text:
eerst = first
daarna = after that / then
toen = then (past)
later = later
's ochtends = in the morning
's middags = in the afternoon
's avonds = in the evening
uiteindelijk = finally

Put them at position 1, verb at position 2 (V2 rule):

Eerst ging ik naar de markt.
Daarna kocht ik kaas.
Toen dronk ik koffie.
Uiteindelijk ging ik naar huis.

This is the skeleton of ANY Dutch story.
Fill in different verbs and you can describe any day.

steps.title: 📋 Describe YOUR Week: Template
steps.text:
Try to describe your own week using this pattern:

Maandag werkte ik. = Monday I worked.
Dinsdag ging ik naar... = Tuesday I went to...
Woensdag heb ik... gedaan. = Wednesday I did...
Donderdag was het druk. = Thursday it was busy.
Vrijdag had ik vrij. = Friday I had off.
Zaterdag ging ik naar de markt. = Saturday I went to the market.
Zondag was ik thuis. = Sunday I was at home.

Verbs you can use:
werken, fietsen, koken, eten, drinken, lezen, kopen,
gaan, komen, lopen, spelen, leren, slapen, zwemmen.

You know ALL of these. Mix and match!

steps.q: The #1 Monday morning question in Dutch offices:
opts: Wat heb je dit weekend gedaan?
steps.ans: Wat heb je dit weekend gedaan?
steps.hint: What did you do this weekend?
First I went to the market

steps.s: {1} kocht ik kaas. {2} ging ik naar huis.

steps.hint: The most common Dutch positive word

steps.q: To describe a day's events in order, use:

steps.title: Gefeliciteerd! A1–A2 Klaar!
steps.desc: You made it. Ten units. Eighty lessons. From 'hallo' to telling stories in the past tense. This final lesson is a celebration — a review of everything, a look at what you can do now, and a peek at what comes next.
goals: See what you can do at A2 level

steps.title: 🏆 What You Can Do Now
steps.text:
At A2 level, you can:

🗣️ Greet, introduce yourself, say where you're from
🔢 Count, tell time, handle dates and prices
🍽️ Order food and drinks, shop at a market
👨‍👩‍👧‍👦 Talk about your family, describe your home
🚲 Give and follow directions, take transport
📅 Describe your daily routine
💪 Say what you can, may, must, and want to do
😊 Express feelings, talk about health
📆 Make plans, discuss the future
📖 Tell stories about the past
🔗 Build complex sentences with subordinate clauses

You know 500+ Dutch words, 15+ verb forms, and the entire
present-past-future tense system.

That's not nothing. That's DUTCH.

steps.title: 📐 Your Grammar Toolkit
steps.text:
Present tense: ik werk, jij werkt, wij werken
Modal verbs: willen, kunnen, mogen, moeten, hoeven
Perfect tense: ik heb gewerkt, ik ben gegaan
Imperfectum: ik werkte, ik ging, ik was
't Kofschip: determines -t/-d and -te/-de
Separable verbs: Ik bel je op. / Ik wil je opbellen.
Reflexive verbs: Ik was me. Ik kleed me aan.
Adjective agreement: de grote fiets, een groot huis
Subordinate clauses: omdat ik moet werken
Relative clauses: de man die hier woont
Possessives: mijn, jouw, zijn, haar, ons/onze, hun
Articles: de, het, een + geen/niet

That is the COMPLETE A1–A2 grammar.
Everything from here builds on this foundation.

steps.q: 'Ik heb gisteren gefietst' is which tense?

steps.s: De man ___ hier woont, heet Jan. (who)
Zij {1} niet te komen. Het {2} een mooie dag.
steps.hint: She doesn't have to come. It was a nice day.

steps.s: Ik fiets ___ naar het werk. (every day)

steps.q: 'Omdat ik moet werken' — the verb is at the end because:
steps.hint: omdat, dat, als → verb to end

steps.q: 'Mag ik de rekening?' — you're asking for:

steps.s: ___ was alles beter. (in the past / back in the day)

opts: The conjugated verb is always in position 2
steps.ans: The conjugated verb is always in position 2
steps.hint: The backbone of Dutch word order

steps.title: 🇳🇱 What Comes Next: B1
steps.text:
At B1, you'll learn:

📝 Passive voice (er wordt gefietst = there is cycling)
📖 More subordinate clause types
💬 Indirect speech (hij zei dat...)
📰 Reading newspaper articles
✍️ Writing longer texts
🗣️ Expressing opinions (ik vind dat...)
📺 Understanding Dutch TV and radio

But that's for later. Right now:

Je hebt het gedaan. Je spreekt Nederlands.
Gefeliciteerd!

🎉 Proost op jou! 🎉

steps.title: 🎉 Een Laatste Woord
steps.text:
You started with 'Hallo' and 'Dag'.

Now you can order a broodje kaas,
tell someone about your family,
cycle through Amsterdam (linguistically),
describe your morning routine,
make plans for the weekend,
and tell the story of your day — in the past tense.

You know what 'gezellig' means (even if you can't translate it).
You know that 'half vijf' is 4:30, not 5:30.
You know that the Dutch eat bread for lunch and call it a meal.
You know that the verb goes to the end in subordinate clauses.

You are no longer a beginner.

Tot ziens — en veel succes!
= Goodbye — and good luck!

🇳🇱
